Check database-specific metrics: buffer cache hit ratio, checkpoint frequency, transaction log contention, replication lag under load
At the OS level: iostat for disk latency, vmstat for context switching, mpstat for CPU balance across cores
For I/O bottlenecks: tune database checkpoint intervals, increase buffer pool, separate data and log on different devices, use O_DIRECT to bypass page cache
commonMistakes: Throwing hardware at the problem without first checking if the database is configured optimally for the workload
Increasing buffer pool without considering NUMA — if buffer pool exceeds local memory, cross-node access kills performance
Ignoring query-level issues: missing indexes and poorly written queries are often the root cause, not system configuration
answerStructure: Identify the bottleneck: use database monitoring (pg_stat_activity, SHOW PROCESSLIST, active query log) and OS metrics
Check CPU: high context switching from lock contention, high system CPU from syscalls (check with perf)
Check I/O: iostat await, database checkpoint frequency, WAL/redo log contention — consider separating log and data devices
Check memory: buffer pool hit ratio, NUMA locality, swap activity
Check concurrency: database connection pool size, thread pool configuration, lock monitoring
Apply tuning: optimize queries and indexes first, then tune database config (shared_buffers, innodb_buffer_pool_size, checkpoint intervals), then OS kernel parameters

question: A network-intensive application shows packet loss. How do you trace it through the entire data path?

keyPoints: Start at layer 1-2: ethtool -S for NIC hardware drops (rx_dropped, rx_missed_errors) and ring buffer overruns
Check the kernel network stack: netstat -s for protocol-level drops (TCP retransmits, checksum errors, listen overflows)
Use dropwatch or kernel tracepoints (skb:kfree_skb) to trace exactly where in the kernel the packet is dropped
Use eBPF with tracepoints at each layer: NIC → GRO → qdisc → netfilter → IP → TCP → socket buffer
commonMistakes: Assuming all packet loss is on the network — many drops occur in the kernel (buffer exhaustion, conntrack limits, firewall rules)
Only checking tcpdump on one end — capture at both the sender and receiver to distinguish network loss from local drops
Ignoring qdisc drops (tc -s qdisc show) — the transmit queue can drop packets under congestion
answerStructure: Confirm loss type: TCP retransmits? ICMP unreachable? Check tcpdump and netstat -s
Check NIC layer: ethtool -S <dev> | grep drop for hardware ring drops, increase ring buffer size if needed
Check kernel drops: dropwatch -l kas to see kernel drop locations, netstat -s for listen overflows and conntrack drops
Check qdisc drops: tc -s qdisc show for backlog and drops on transmit queue
Check iptables/nftables: do any rules drop packets (policy or explicit rules)? Check conntrack table full
Check application: is the socket buffer full (SO_RCVBUF)? Is the application reading fast enough (epoll/select)?

question: The OOM killer is killing processes even though memory appears available. How do you investigate?

keyPoints: Check dmesg for OOM killer output — it shows the OOM score, memory stats, and which process was selected and why
Verify memory overcommit: vm.overcommit_memory=0 (heuristic) may overcommit; vm.overcommit_ratio controls how much overcommit is allowed
Check for memory cgroup limits: if the process is in a cgroup with memory.max set, the cgroup OOM killer triggers even when system memory is available
Examine oom_score_adj — processes with positive adjustment are more likely to be killed; system services often have negative adjustments
commonMistakes: Assuming OOM kills mean the system is out of memory — it could be a cgroup limit or memory.max constraint
Not checking low memory specifically — OOM killer triggers when low memory is exhausted even if total free memory is high
Disabling overcommit (vm.overcommit_memory=2) blindly — it prevents overcommit but fork() may fail (cannot allocate memory) for legitimate processes
answerStructure: Examine dmesg OOM logs: identify which process was killed, memory statistics at the time, and OOM score details
Check system memory: free -h, cat /proc/meminfo (especially MemAvailable, not just MemFree), /proc/zoneinfo for lowmem
Check overcommit: cat /proc/sys/vm/overcommit_memory and /proc/sys/vm/overcommit_ratio
Check cgroup limits: if process is containerized, check cgroup memory.max, memory.current
Check OOM score: cat /proc/<PID>/oom_score, /proc/<PID>/oom_score_adj for both the killed process and memory hogs
Apply fix: increase cgroup limit, reduce overcommit, add swap, fix memory leak, or adjust oom_score_adj

question: CPU soft lockups occur intermittently on a production server. How do you debug the cause?

keyPoints: A soft lockup means a CPU core did not handle scheduling ticks for more than 20 seconds — check dmesg for lockup detection messages with the offending CPU and stack trace
Common causes: kernel code running with interrupts disabled for too long, infinite loops in kernel modules, filesystem hangs, or driver bugs
Use the NMI watchdog (hard lockup detector) to differentiate between hard and soft lockups — soft lockups have interrupts still enabled but no scheduling
Use ftrace or perf with tracepoint sched:sched_switch to see what was running on the offending CPU before the lockup
commonMistakes: Confusing soft lockups with high load — soft lockup means kernel code is stuck (interrupts or preemption disabled), not just high CPU utilization
Ignoring hardware issues — faulty hardware (especially storage controllers) can cause interrupt starvation at the firmware level
Not enabling lockup detection config (CONFIG_LOCKUP_DETECTOR, CONFIG_HARDLOCKUP_DETECTOR) in the kernel
answerStructure: Collect evidence: dmesg | grep -i lockup shows the CPU, duration, and stack trace of the stuck context
Check the stack trace: which kernel function is the CPU stuck in? Look for filesystem code, driver code, or memory management
Use perf: perf record -g -e cycles -a during the lockup (or use NMI-based sampling if possible)
Check hardware: firmware versions, storage controller logs, ACPI power states, CPU microcode
Mitigate while debugging: isolate the workload to different CPUs, try disabling C-states, update firmware
If it is a known kernel bug: reproduce on a newer kernel or apply specific patch; report upstream with stack trace

question: Filesystem corruption is detected on an ext4 volume. Describe your recovery approach and how you would prevent recurrence.

keyPoints: First, unmount the filesystem if possible and take a block-level backup with dd or ddrescue before any recovery attempt
Run fsck.ext4 -f -y on the unmounted filesystem to repair structural damage; the -n flag can first check what repairs are needed without applying them
For severe corruption (fsck cannot repair), use ddrescue to recover data, then try debugfs to extract files manually from the raw block device
After recovery, investigate root cause: hardware (disk SMART errors, cable, controller), kernel bug, power loss, or filesystem bug
commonMistakes: Running fsck on a mounted filesystem — it will cause further corruption and fail with a warning
Using -y (auto yes) without first using -n to see what changes will be made — -y can delete data that might be recoverable with manual intervention
Remounting the filesystem without identifying root cause — it may corrupt again immediately
answerStructure: Stop write activity: unmount the filesystem, or remount read-only if unmount fails
Create a backup: dd if=/dev/sdb1 of=/backup/sdb1.img bs=4M conv=noerror,sync to preserve the current state
Run fsck in read-only mode: fsck.ext4 -fn /dev/sdb1 to see what errors exist without fixing
Run fsck with repair: fsck.ext4 -fy /dev/sdb1 to fix structural issues
If fsck fails: try debugfs to extract critical files, or use ext4magic for journal-based recovery
Identify root cause: check SMART (smartctl -a), kernel logs (dmesg | grep sd), power supply, and verify cables
Prevent recurrence: switch to XFS (better crash recovery), enable periodic scrub (xfs_scrub/ext4 scrub), add monitoring, use UPS

question: A service fails to scale past 16 cores even though CPU utilization is moderate. Investigate and identify the limitation.

keyPoints: Use perf top to check for high kernel spinlock contention (spin_lock, mutex_lock), cache line bouncing (perf c2c), or RCU stall warnings
Check NUMA topology: the application may be running across multiple NUMA nodes, incurring remote memory access penalties as it scales past the cores in one node
Use flame graphs with frame pointers to identify serialization points — locks, atomic operations, or global counters that become contended
The Amdahl's Law bottleneck might be in a shared data structure, a single-threaded event loop, or a kernel-level limit like a global lock in the network stack or filesystem
commonMistakes: Assuming adding more cores will always improve performance — lock contention, cache coherence traffic, and NUMA penalties reverse scaling at some point
Not profiling with --call-graph — without stack traces, you cannot identify the function holding the contended lock
Ignoring the kernel's contribution — the contention may be in a kernel subsystem (e.g., network stack, memory allocator, filesystem)
answerStructure: Characterize the scaling: does throughput plateau or decrease past 16 cores? Graph the scaling curve
Profile CPU utilization: is user time dropping and system time increasing as cores increase (sign of syscall contention)?
Use perf c2c to detect cache line contention (false sharing) — threads writing to different fields in the same cache line
Use perf record -e cycles:u -g and generate flame graphs to identify hot functions and lock contention
Check NUMA: numastat -p <PID> shows memory allocation across nodes; aim for all memory and threads on same node
Identify and fix: use per-thread data structures, read-copy-update (RCU), lock striping, or lock-free algorithms

question: A kernel panic occurs under a specific workload. How do you bisect the kernel to identify the offending commit?

keyPoints: Capture the panic details: use netconsole or serial console to get the full oops/panic log including register state, stack trace, and ksym addresses
Use kdump/kexec to capture a crash dump (vmcore) and analyze with crash utility offline for detailed post-mortem
Reproduce the crash reliably on a specific kernel version before attempting to bisect — the workload must trigger the panic consistently
Use git bisect over the kernel git tree, building and testing each bisect point until the bad commit is identified
commonMistakes: Starting a bisect without a reliable reproduction test — false positives waste hours of bisect iterations
Not using a scripted test (kernel test harness) — manual testing across dozens of kernel builds is error-prone
Bisecting blind: not understanding the crash signature and which subsystem is likely responsible
answerStructure: Set up crash capture: configure kdump (crashkernel= parameter, kexec-tools) to capture vmcore on panic
Analyze the vmcore with crash utility: bt (backtrace), log (kernel log), ps (processes), vm (virtual memory) to understand the crash context
Identify the subsystem: does the crash occur in networking, filesystem, memory management, scheduler?
Create a minimal reproducer: if possible, reduce the workload to the smallest trigger (single syscall, specific hardware operation)
Run git bisect: mark known good (e.g., stable tag), known bad (current kernel), and let bisect find the commit
After finding the commit: review the patch for the bug, and either revert, apply a fix, or report upstream with the bisect result

question: Describe a time you drove a fundamental infrastructure change that significantly impacted the organization.

keyPoints: Describe the motivation: what problem necessitated the change (scalability, cost, reliability, security)
Explain how you built consensus: RFC process, proof of concept, stakeholder buy-in, executive sponsorship
Detail the migration strategy: how did you move from old to new without downtime? Phased rollout, parallel run, feature flags
Quantify the impact: cost savings (e.g., 40% reduction), performance improvement (2x throughput), or reliability (99.9% to 99.99%)
commonMistakes: Describing a change that was trivial or had minimal impact
Not mentioning how you handled resistance or technical disagreements
Failing to discuss rollback planning or what happened when things went wrong
answerStructure: Describe the old infrastructure and its limitations (technical debt, scaling ceiling, cost)
Explain the proposed new system and why it was the right choice (research, POC, comparison)
Walk through the rollout strategy: how you ensured zero downtime, which teams were involved
Discuss challenges you faced: unexpected issues, rollbacks, performance regressions
Share the measurable outcomes: cost, performance, reliability metrics improved
Reflect on lessons learned: what you would do differently, advice for similar transformations

question: How do you balance the need for reliability with the need for velocity in deployments?

keyPoints: Implement deployment safety nets: feature flags, canary deployments, blue-green deployments, and automated rollback
Use error budgets: define SLOs, track error budget burn rate, and slow down deployments when budget is depleted
Build quality into the pipeline: automated testing (unit, integration, chaos), security scanning, and compliance checks before production
Foster a blameless culture where post-mortems lead to system improvements rather than process slowdowns
commonMistakes: Adding manual approval gates for every change — this creates bottlenecks without improving safety
Implementing so many safety checks that deployments take days — velocity matters for security fixes and business responsiveness
Relying on process instead of automation — manual steps are where mistakes happen
answerStructure: Define your philosophy: reliability and velocity are not opposites; automation enables both
Describe safety mechanisms: feature flags for gradual rollout, automated canary analysis with metrics comparison
Explain the error budget concept: SLO of 99.9% allows 8.6 hours of downtime per year — that is your budget for risk
Discuss deployment frequency: aim for small, frequent, reversible changes rather than large, risky releases
Cover the feedback loop: how you monitor deployment health and trigger automated rollback
Give a real example: successfully deploying 50+ times per day while maintaining 99.99% availability

question: Tell me about a major incident post-mortem you led. How did you drive systemic improvements?

keyPoints: Structure the post-mortem: timeline of events, detection, response, root cause, contributing factors, and action items
Focus on systemic factors, not individual blame — ask what system conditions allowed the error to happen
Prioritize action items by impact: immediate mitigations, short-term fixes, long-term architectural changes
Track action items to completion and verify their effectiveness with follow-up reviews
commonMistakes: Making the post-mortem a blame session — this discourages transparency and future reporting
Generating a long list of action items without prioritizing — no team can fix 50 things at once
Writing the post-mortem in isolation — gather input from all involved parties for a complete picture
answerStructure: Describe the incident: what happened, severity, duration, customer impact
Explain how you organized the post-mortem: who was involved, timeline construction, data gathering
Walk through the root cause analysis: the 5 Whys, contributing factors, and system vulnerabilities
Discuss the action items: which fixes were applied immediately, which became roadmap items
Share how you tracked progress: regular check-ins on action items, verify fixes prevented recurrence
Reflect on the culture impact: how the blameless post-mortem improved incident reporting and team trust

question: How do you build a culture of operational excellence within an engineering team?

keyPoints: Lead by example: prioritize operational work (monitoring, documentation, automation) alongside feature development
Institute regular rituals: weekly operations review, rotating on-call with clear responsibilities, post-mortems after every significant incident
Invest in automation: reduce toil so engineers have time and energy for improvement work
Measure and share operational metrics: error budgets, MTTR, deployment frequency, and make them visible to the whole team
commonMistakes: Treating operations as separate from development — operational excellence requires Dev and Ops collaboration
Recognizing only feature delivery, not reliability improvements — operational work must be celebrated
Implementing processes without buy-in — people support what they help create; involve the team in designing on-call rotations and SLOs
answerStructure: Define what operational excellence means: reliability, observability, incident response, and continuous improvement
Describe team rituals: daily standup ops check, weekly error budget review, monthly incident drill
Explain on-call culture: fair rotation, clear escalation paths, post-incident decompression, blameless reviews
Discuss quality gates: production readiness reviews, chaos engineering experiments, load testing
Cover knowledge sharing: runbook reviews, brown bag sessions, cross-training to avoid bus factor
Share a specific outcome: how a reliability culture reduced P0 incidents or improved MTTR

question: Describe a situation where you had to make a critical decision with incomplete information. How did you proceed?

keyPoints: Acknowledge the uncertainty and identify what information is most critical to the decision
Use a structured approach: list assumptions, evaluate best/worst-case scenarios, estimate probabilities
De-risk the decision: choose reversible options where possible, or make incremental commitments that preserve optionality
Communicate the decision and its rationale transparently to stakeholders, noting what is certain vs uncertain
commonMistakes: Paralyzing the team by waiting for perfect information — in production incidents, you must act on incomplete data
Making a decision without articulating assumptions and risks to stakeholders
Failing to revisit the decision when new information becomes available
answerStructure: Describe the situation: what was at stake, why was information incomplete (incident urgency, system complexity)
Explain your decision framework: what criteria did you use, what were the key tradeoffs
Detail the decision: which option you chose and why, what assumptions you made
Discuss how you managed risk: what safety nets or monitoring did you put in place
Reflect on the outcome: was the decision correct? What did you learn about decision-making under uncertainty?

question: How do you approach technical debt at the infrastructure level? When do you prioritize paying it down vs shipping new features?

keyPoints: Classify technical debt: principal (cost to fix now) vs interest (ongoing cost of not fixing — slower deploys, more incidents, higher toil)
Use a cost-benefit framework: quantify the interest payments (engineer hours lost per week, incident frequency) and compare to fix cost
Adopt the rule of thirds: one-third of time for new features, one-third for fixing debt, one-third for improving the platform
Create visibility: maintain a technical debt register with estimated effort and impact, discuss in quarterly planning
commonMistakes: Allowing all infrastructure work to be deprioritized as technical debt — some is essential maintenance that keeps the system running
Fixing everything at once without understanding the cost-to-benefit ratio of each item
Not measuring the impact of debt — without data, it is hard to justify investment to stakeholders
answerStructure: Define infrastructure debt: outdated tooling, manual processes, deprecated dependencies, missing automation, capacity risks
Describe how you surface debt: from incident post-mortems, on-call feedback, deploy friction, engineer surveys
Explain your prioritization framework: impact on velocity, reliability risk, cost, and alignment with strategic goals
Discuss how you negotiate with product teams: use error budgets and reliability metrics to justify infrastructure investment
Give an example: paying down a specific debt and the measurable improvement (reduced deploy time, fewer incidents)
Reflect on balance: how you decide when debt becomes critical enough to stop feature work

question: Tell me about a time you influenced organizational change beyond your immediate team.

keyPoints: Describe the change: a new practice, tool, or process adopted beyond your team (e.g., containerization, SLO framework, on-call standards)
Explain your influence strategy: build a coalition of supporters, demonstrate value with a POC, present data-driven arguments to leadership
Show persistence: organizational change takes time — multiple presentations, iterations, and stakeholder management
Highlight the outcome: how the organization operates differently because of the change you drove
commonMistakes: Claiming credit for a change that was already in progress or mandated from the top
Describing a change that only affected your team, not the broader organization
Not addressing resistance — every organizational change has skeptics; how you handled them matters
answerStructure: Describe the problem that affected multiple teams (e.g., no consistent on-call process, unreliable deploys)
Explain your proposal: what change you advocated for and why it would help beyond your team
Detail your influence tactics: built a cross-team working group, created a compelling POC, presented to leadership
Discuss challenges: how you addressed skepticism, adapted the proposal based on feedback
Share the organizational impact: how many teams adopted it, what metrics improved organization-wide
Reflect on what you learned about driving change in a large organization

question: How do you evaluate and adopt new technologies for infrastructure? What is your decision framework?

keyPoints: Start with the problem, not the solution: what pain point or capability gap are you trying to address?
Evaluate candidates on multiple axes: maturity (community, production deployments), operational complexity, learning curve, vendor lock-in, total cost
Run a structured POC: define success criteria, test under realistic conditions, measure against baseline
Assess adoption risk: can it be introduced incrementally (sidecar, API compatibility)? Is there a rollback path?
commonMistakes: Falling in love with a technology before understanding the problem — shiny object syndrome
Not involving the operations team early — they will maintain it, their buy-in is critical
Ignoring total cost of ownership: licensing, training, migration effort, ongoing maintenance overhead
Adopting bleeding-edge technology without evaluating maturity and community support
answerStructure: Describe the problem: what gap in the current infrastructure prompted the evaluation
Explain the evaluation criteria: scalability, reliability, ecosystem maturity, community health, learning curve, cost
Walk through the evaluation process: research (papers, talks, case studies), shortlist, POC with defined success metrics
Discuss how you built consensus: share findings in tech talks, involve stakeholders from affected teams
Cover the adoption plan: phased rollout, parallel run, monitoring, rollback plan, team training
Give a concrete example: a technology you evaluated and adopted, and the outcome
